definition: Endotoxin (lipopolysaccharide; LPS) is a component of Gram-negative bacterial cell walls and can be present in indoor and agricultural environments. Some cohort studies suggest early-life microbial exposures may be associated with lower rates of allergic disease in certain contexts, but effects are complex and environment-dependent. GENARCH describes this evidence in a neutral, educational framing.
name: Endotoxin (Environmental Microbial Products)

references.title: Endotoxin exposure and allergic disease: complex context-dependent effects

sensitive_windows.mechanism_rationale: Immune maturation occurs rapidly in early life; microbial exposures during this period are hypothesized to influence allergic sensitization trajectories in some settings.

systems_affected.mechanism_summary: Microbial products can engage innate immune receptors (e.g., TLR4), potentially shaping immune development; directionality may differ by timing, dose, and co-exposures.
